events.title: The Permanent Inauguration
events.scene: The crowd on the Mall is the biggest in history, you are assured, and for once nobody dares dispute it. Your second term is winding down, but the music feels less like a finale and more like an overture. Aides float a phrase you find delicious: 'the beginning of the golden century.'
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation. U.S. presidents are limited to two terms by the 22nd Amendment; this scenario imagines a leader and movement openly reframing term limits as negotiable, a real rhetorical trend pushed past its constitutional boundary.

choices.text: Float the idea that 'the people' may demand a third term
choices.outcome: The base roars, the courts mutter, and the word 'unprecedented' loses all meaning. You like it that way.

choices.text: Publicly honor the limit while quietly grooming a successor
choices.outcome: You play the statesman on camera and the dynast off it. The succession, you decide, will simply keep the name.

choices.text: Promise a free, fair election and a peaceful transfer of power
choices.outcome: Half your advisors think you have gone soft. Historians, you suspect, will think otherwise.

events.title: Staffed With Believers
events.scene: The career bureaucrats are gone, reassigned, or retired into silence. Every agency is now run by someone who applauds at the right moments. A new loyalty review board screens appointees for 'alignment,' which everyone understands to mean alignment with you.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real proposals (such as reclassifying federal workers to make them easier to remove) that would expand a president's control over the civil service. The full purge depicted here is imagined.

choices.text: Replace anyone insufficiently devoted with a true believer
choices.outcome: The machinery of state now hums in one key: yours. Dissent doesn't get fired so much as it ceases to exist.

choices.text: Keep a handful of competent skeptics for the hard problems
choices.outcome: A few grown-ups remain in the room. They are useful, occasionally annoying, and always reminded who signs the checks.

choices.text: Strengthen merit protections and depoliticize the agencies
choices.outcome: You bind your own hands and call it strength. The bureaucracy, oddly, starts working better than it has in years.

events.title: Owning the Signal
events.scene: Your family's media venture has swallowed a streaming giant and a social platform in one weekend. The chyrons agree with you now, all of them. A new 'Patriot Algorithm' decides what eighty million phones see at breakfast, and it has excellent taste.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real trends of political figures owning media platforms and the documented power of recommendation algorithms to shape public opinion. The scale of state-aligned control here is imagined.

choices.text: Tune the algorithm to amplify praise and bury criticism
choices.outcome: Reality becomes a feed, and you are its editor-in-chief. The trending tab is, at last, completely under control.

choices.text: Skip the propaganda and just monetize the attention
choices.outcome: You let the platform print money instead of slogans. The accountants weep with joy; the editorialists, less so.

choices.text: Open the algorithm to public audit and break it into rival networks
choices.outcome: You give the public a window into the machine that shapes them. The trolls revolt; the historians take notes.

events.title: The Cloud Bends the Knee
events.scene: The five companies that run the world's compute, search, and phones invite themselves to dinner. They want exemptions, contracts, and a friendly regulator; you want their data, their reach, and their gratitude. Everyone leaves the table holding a little more leverage than they walked in with.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real and growing entanglement between governments and a handful of dominant technology firms over cloud infrastructure, content moderation, and data access. The explicit fusion of state and platform here is dramatized.

choices.text: Trade regulatory cover for a direct pipe into their data
choices.outcome: The surveillance and the search bar now answer to the same desk. Privacy becomes a setting you can quietly switch off.

choices.text: Launch the biggest antitrust crusade in a century
choices.outcome: You shatter the giants into competing shards. The lobbyists wail; the market, blinking, rediscovers competition.

choices.text: Take their money in taxes and leave their power intact
choices.outcome: You tax the colossi and call it a day. They pay, smile, and keep right on growing.

events.title: The Machine That Never Sleeps
events.scene: A frontier AI lab unveils a model that can draft policy, predict unrest, and write speeches in your cadence better than your own writers. They offer it to you as a governing tool: a tireless advisor that reads every cable and never leaks. The demo ends with the model suggesting, gently, three people it thinks you should remove.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real rapid advances in large AI models and early experiments using them for policy analysis and decision support. Handing core governance to an opaque model, as imagined here, raises accountability and alignment risks far beyond current practice.

choices.text: Let the model triage your enemies and optimize your grip
choices.outcome: The machine governs and you preside. Its recommendations are always reasonable, which is exactly what worries the few who notice.

choices.text: Pour the budget into open, safety-first public AI research
choices.outcome: You make the breakthrough a commons instead of a crown. The lab grumbles; the species, eventually, benefits.

choices.text: Slap a strict license on it and keep humans in the loop
choices.outcome: You leash the thing before it leashes you. Progress slows; the night watch sleeps a little easier.

events.title: The City With Your Name
events.scene: Ground breaks on 'Liberty Prime,' a gleaming planned megacity rising from the desert, financed by a coalition of grateful billionaires and a sovereign fund or three. Golden towers, an airport, and a monument shaped suspiciously like you. The ribbon is a mile long.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real-world freedom-city and charter-city proposals and large state-backed megaprojects. The presidential vanity-capital framing is imagined satire.

choices.text: Demand the biggest, tallest, most golden version possible
choices.outcome: The skyline becomes a signature. Engineers fret about the budget; you remind them that legends are never cheap.

choices.text: Let others build it and just license your name to it
choices.outcome: You take the upside and none of the rebar. The name does the heavy lifting; it always has.

choices.text: Redirect the funds to housing, transit, and clean water nationwide
choices.outcome: No monument bears your face, but a million faucets run clean. It is, your critics admit, a strange kind of triumph.

events.title: The Family Line
events.scene: At a primetime dinner you seat your children like a royal table, each handed a portfolio: Don Jr. gets the party, Ivanka the brand, Barron the future. The pundits call it nepotism; you call it succession planning. Somewhere a constitutional scholar quietly retires.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real instances of political families building multi-generational influence (Kennedys, Bushes, Clintons) pushed toward an explicitly dynastic, near-monarchical model. The formal handoff depicted is imagined.

choices.text: Anoint Don Jr. as heir and build the dynasty openly
choices.outcome: The name becomes a title. The family kisses the ring, and the ring, you note, fits the whole bloodline.

choices.text: Pass the brand and the throne to Ivanka, the favorite
choices.outcome: Camera-ready and ruthless, she takes the portfolio without blinking. The dynasty, you decide, will photograph beautifully.

choices.text: Keep every heir hungry by never naming the favorite
choices.outcome: You dangle the crown and watch them compete. Cruel, effective, and excellent television.

choices.text: Tell your children to earn it at the ballot box like anyone else
choices.outcome: The family sulks; the republic exhales. A bloodline, you remind them, is not a birthright to a country.

events.title: The Indispensable Man
events.scene: World leaders no longer call to negotiate; they call to ask permission. A summit in your golden megacity turns into a coronation as prime ministers queue for a handshake and a photo. The phrase 'leader of the free world' has quietly dropped the word 'free.'
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from the real outsized gravitational pull of U.S. presidents on global affairs. The depicted tilt toward personal, unilateral global dominance is imagined and gated to escalate when domestic power has been consolidated.

choices.text: Reframe alliances as tribute and make them pay for protection
choices.outcome: The world pays up, resentfully. You have turned diplomacy into a protection racket with very good catering.

choices.text: Broker a grand bargain that puts you at the center of everything
choices.outcome: Every treaty now routes through you. Indispensable is the word; irreplaceable is the goal.

choices.text: Surprise everyone and hand real power to a council of nations
choices.outcome: You give away the gavel and keep the goodwill. The strongmen are baffled; the small nations, for once, are heard.

events.title: The Other Strongmen
events.scene: A handful of rival great powers have their own strongmen, their own golden palaces, their own algorithms. China flatters you in public and probes you in private; Russia sends a gift the size of a yacht; a newly unified EU sends a fleet a little too close to your shipping lanes.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real multipolar geopolitical competition among major powers and personalist leaders, plus ongoing debates about deeper European political integration. Specific rivalries and a fully federated EU are fictionalized.

choices.text: Forge a cynical pact with China and Russia carving the world into spheres
choices.outcome: You and your fellow autocrats divide the map over dinner. The smaller nations are not invited; they are the menu.

choices.text: Play the rivals against each other and keep them off balance
choices.outcome: You keep every rival guessing and every alliance temporary. Chaos, properly managed, is just leverage.

choices.text: Propose a binding concert of powers to ban arms races and famine
choices.outcome: You turn the rivals into partners by giving them something to lose together. Even the cynics admit it might hold.

events.title: The New Space Race
events.scene: China lands a permanent crew on the Moon and plants a flag the size of a tennis court, broadcast in 8K to a billion people. Your generals call it humiliation; your industrialists call it opportunity. SpaceX has a rocket on the pad and a price tag with a lot of zeros, and the whole world is suddenly looking up.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real renewed great-power competition in spaceflight, including crewed lunar programs and rapid commercial launch development. The specific milestones and timeline are dramatized.

choices.text: Pour billions into an orbital weapons program to dominate space
choices.outcome: The high ground becomes the highest ground. Rivals scramble to match you, and the heavens acquire a target list.

choices.text: Triple NASA's budget for a peaceful Mars-and-beyond mission
choices.outcome: You bet on wonder instead of warheads. A generation of kids picks up telescopes; a generation of engineers picks up the slack.

choices.text: Hand the whole frontier to private contractors for a cut
choices.outcome: Space becomes a market and you take a royalty on the cosmos. The flags up there now have logos.

events.title: Eyes on the Void
events.scene: With a science renaissance underway, astronomers pitch a constellation of giant deep-space telescopes, an array that could read the atmosphere of a planet a thousand light-years off. They speak of biosignatures, of mapping the dark, of finally answering the oldest question. The budget is enormous; the implications are larger.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real next-generation observatory proposals (successors to JWST, large ground arrays, exoplanet atmosphere characterization). The scale and capability described run ahead of currently funded programs.

choices.text: Fund the full deep-space telescope array, no expense spared
choices.outcome: Mirrors the size of stadiums unfold in the dark. For the first time, humanity has eyes big enough to be surprised.

choices.text: Fund a single flagship telescope and call it prudent
choices.outcome: One great eye opens instead of a hundred. It is enough to see something out there worth a longer look.

choices.text: Defer the program and spend the money on Earth's poorest
choices.outcome: The stars can wait, you decide; the hungry cannot. The astronomers grumble, then quietly agree.

events.title: The Question Worth Funding
events.scene: A coalition of scientists asks for something almost no leader has ever fully backed: a serious, sustained search for intelligent life among the stars. Listening arrays, transmission protocols, a standing committee for what to do if anyone answers. They cannot promise a signal. They can only promise that you will have looked.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real, historically underfunded SETI efforts and recent technosignature initiatives. A government making the search a national priority, as imagined here, would be unprecedented.

choices.text: Make the search for alien intelligence a permanent national mission
choices.outcome: You commit a nation to listening. Critics call it a moonshot for moonbeams; you call it the most serious question there is.

choices.text: Greenlight a small, quiet pilot and see what comes back
choices.outcome: A handful of dishes turn skyward on a shoestring. It is not much, but the universe, you note, only has to answer once.

choices.text: Laugh it off as money better spent on Earthly glory
choices.outcome: You kill the dish before it listens. Somewhere, a frequency hums on, unheard, with no one assigned to care.

events.title: Something Answers
events.scene: Deep in the data from your arrays, a pattern emerges that is not noise: structured, repeating, unmistakably made. The scientists are shaking. The signal is faint and far, decades old at least, but it is real. The most important decision of human history is sitting on your desk, and for once the whole species, not just your base, is waiting on your word.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation imagining a positive technosignature detection from sustained deep-space observation and SETI investment. No such confirmed signal exists; this dramatizes the protocols and stakes that real detection frameworks contemplate.

choices.text: Convene the world and broadcast a message of peace on humanity's behalf
choices.outcome: You speak for Earth, and you choose the better words. Whatever is out there, it will hear a species at its best.

choices.text: Classify the signal and use the secret as leverage over rivals
choices.outcome: You bury the most important fact in history in a vault. Knowledge is power, and this knowledge could move the world; so you hide it.

choices.text: Tell no one yet and quietly fund decades of careful study
choices.outcome: You choose patience over spectacle. The signal keeps its secrets a while longer, and so, for now, do you.

events.title: The Forever Project
events.scene: A team of biotech founders pitches you on something better than a third term: a fourth, fifth, and sixth decade in power. Senolytics, gene therapy, blood from the young and grateful. They promise you will outlast not just your rivals but your own obituary.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real, heavily funded longevity and anti-aging research (senolytics, cellular reprogramming, parabiosis experiments). The promise of meaningful life extension on this timeline is far from established and is dramatized here.

choices.text: Pour a fortune into radical life-extension research for yourself
choices.outcome: The labs go to work and your doctors get nervous. If power corrupts, you intend to be corrupt for a very long time.

choices.text: Fund the research but pledge the cures will be free for everyone
choices.outcome: You chase immortality and refuse to hoard it. A longer life, you decide, should not be a luxury good.

choices.text: Settle for the best conventional care money can buy
choices.outcome: No miracle science, just an army of specialists. Mortal, perhaps, but extremely well-maintained.

events.title: The Golden Engine
events.scene: A wave of automation and cheap energy has the economy roaring louder than any in living memory. Output is up, the markets are euphoric, and a tidal wave of new wealth is sloshing through the system. The only question, your advisors say carefully, is who gets to keep it.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real debates over AI-driven productivity gains and their distribution, plus historical patterns of booms concentrating wealth at the top. The magnitude and timing of the boom are dramatized.

choices.text: Funnel the windfall to your allies and the donor class
choices.outcome: The yachts get longer and the loyalty deepens. A rising tide, it turns out, lifts mostly the boats you own.

choices.text: Pass a universal dividend that shares the boom with every citizen
choices.outcome: Every household gets a check and a stake. The donors are furious; the streets, for once, are grateful.

choices.text: Bank the surplus in a sovereign wealth fund for hard times
choices.outcome: You stash the boom away for the bust everyone pretends won't come. Prudent, dull, and quietly wise.

events.title: The Commonwealth Turn
events.scene: Years of sharing the wealth have changed the country's mood and your own. Movements you once mocked now cite you as an ally; economists speak of a 'post-scarcity pivot.' A coalition arrives with a radical proposal: dissolve the old hierarchies entirely and build something genuinely common.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real proposals for universal basic income, public ownership of automation dividends, and post-scarcity economic theory. The full societal transformation depicted is utopian speculation, not a forecast.

choices.text: Lead the transition to a moneyless, post-scarcity commonwealth
choices.outcome: You preside over the end of want and the beginning of something with no name yet. The history books will need a new chapter.

choices.text: Stop short of utopia and lock in a strong welfare state
choices.outcome: Not paradise, but a floor no one falls through. You trade the grand dream for a sturdy, humane compromise.

choices.text: Decide it has gone far enough and consolidate your own position
choices.outcome: You slam the brakes before the experiment runs away. The radicals call it betrayal; you call it staying in charge.

events.title: The Jobless Recovery
events.scene: In eighteen months, automation has erased a third of the country's white-collar work. The unemployment lines are full of lawyers and accountants who never thought it could be them. The markets are up; the mood is feral. Something has to give, and everyone is looking at you.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real and ongoing concerns about AI and automation displacing knowledge work, accelerated here to a single dramatic shock. The pace and scale are dramatized beyond current evidence.

choices.text: Brand the displaced as agitators and send in the forces
choices.outcome: Order returns at the point of a baton. The cameras find new things to show; the grievances find new places to hide.

choices.text: Launch a massive jobs guarantee and retraining program
choices.outcome: You meet the machine age with a human plan. It is expensive, slow, and the only thing that actually works.

choices.text: Insist the market will sort it out and do nothing
choices.outcome: You wait for an invisible hand that never reaches down. The market sorts it out, eventually, cruelly.

events.title: The Summer of Fire
events.scene: Three megafires, two failed harvests, and a hurricane that parks over a coastline for a week. The climate everyone argued about has stopped arguing back. Refugees move inland by the millions, and your golden megacity's air conditioning bill could fund a small nation.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real, observed trends in extreme weather, crop stress, and climate-driven displacement, compressed into a single severe season. The specific events are dramatized.

choices.text: Build climate fortresses for the loyal and seal the borders
choices.outcome: The chosen ride out the heat behind tall walls and cold air. Outside, the world bakes, and the walls grow taller every year.

choices.text: Launch a green Manhattan Project to decarbonize and adapt
choices.outcome: You throw the full weight of a superpower at the sky. It is the war your generation was actually born to fight.

choices.text: Deny it is happening and double down on cheap fossil fuels
choices.outcome: You declare the weather fake and the wells open. The thermometer, indifferent to your ratings, keeps climbing.

events.title: The Great Unwind
events.scene: The golden boom finally cracks. A bubble bursts somewhere in the automation markets and the contagion spreads in hours. Banks wobble, pensions evaporate, and the word 'depression' creeps back into polite conversation. The fund you may or may not have built is suddenly the most important number on Earth.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real boom-bust financial cycles and systemic risk in highly leveraged, automated markets. The specific crash trigger is fictional.

choices.text: Bail out your allies and let ordinary savers drown
choices.outcome: The right people are made whole and the rest are made examples. Capitalism for the poor, socialism for your friends.

choices.text: Backstop households and small businesses first
choices.outcome: You catch the families before the bankers. It costs more, polls worse with donors, and is unmistakably the right call.

choices.text: Let the market purge itself with no intervention
choices.outcome: You stand back and call the wreckage a cleansing. The cleansing takes a decade and a generation of savings with it.

events.title: The Next One
events.scene: A novel pathogen jumps continents before the first headline lands. Hospitals fill, supply chains snap, and the country looks to you with the muscle memory of a trauma it swore it had learned from. The labs that survived your budgets are sequencing it now. Your move could save millions or repeat history.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real pandemic-preparedness concerns and the documented difficulty of coordinated public-health response. The specific outbreak is fictional.

choices.text: Downplay it, control the data, and protect the markets
choices.outcome: You manage the message instead of the microbe. The graphs you hide keep climbing where no one can edit them.

choices.text: Trust the scientists, fund the vaccines, and share them globally
choices.outcome: You lead with the lab and the truth. The curve bends, the world borrows your playbook, and millions simply live.

choices.text: Seal the borders, hoard the cure, and ride it out alone
choices.outcome: You wall off the nation and the cure. It works at home and breeds resentment abroad, where the variants are already mutating.

events.title: The Last Aquifer
events.scene: A drought belt now spans three continents, and the wars are no longer about oil but about water, lithium, and arable dirt. A rival masses troops near a basin your industries cannot live without. The generals want a preemptive strike; the diplomats want a deal; the planet wants a drink.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real and projected conflicts over water, critical minerals, and arable land under climate stress. The specific standoff is fictional.

choices.text: Strike first and seize the basin by force
choices.outcome: You take the water and the world's water with it. The basin is yours; so, now, is the war that comes after.

choices.text: Broker an international stewardship treaty for the basin
choices.outcome: You turn a casus belli into a commons. Engineers, not soldiers, get the assignment, and everyone drinks.

choices.text: Pour money into desalination and synthetic alternatives instead
choices.outcome: You decide the way out of scarcity is through it. The labs go to work; the troops, for now, go home.

events.title: The Knives Come Out
events.scene: With a dynasty declared, the family that smiled for the cameras is now circling each other with real menace. One heir controls the media, another the security services, a third the money. A leaked memo suggests one of them is tired of waiting for nature to take its course.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real historical patterns of succession struggles within dynastic and authoritarian regimes, where the absence of clear rules invites internal conflict. The specific intrigue is dramatized.

choices.text: Eliminate the rivals and crown the most ruthless heir
choices.outcome: The throne is secured in the oldest way there is. The dynasty endures, lighter by a few inconvenient relatives.

choices.text: Write real rules so the country isn't hostage to your family
choices.outcome: You bind even your own blood to a process. It is the most kingly thing you have ever done: limiting the king.

choices.text: Step down and hand power to an elected council instead
choices.outcome: You walk away from the throne while you still can. The family is stunned; the republic, improbably, gets a second life.

events.title: Eyeball to Eyeball
events.scene: A rival fleet shadows yours in contested waters and refuses to blink. Your generals lay out options on a golden table; one folder is thicker, darker, and labeled with a word nobody likes to say aloud. The hotline is ringing. The world is watching you decide.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real nuclear-deterrence brinkmanship and historical near-misses (such as the Cuban Missile Crisis). The depicted standoff is fictional and dramatized; choosing escalation seeds a catastrophic chain into a later era.

choices.text: Go to the brink and dare them to flinch first
choices.outcome: You raise the alert level and the temperature with it. The whole planet holds its breath, and a darker era leans in to listen.

choices.text: Pull back at the last second and pocket the bluff
choices.outcome: You blink first and call it strength, loudly. The fleets withdraw; the apocalypse files itself back in the drawer, for now.

choices.text: Stun the world by proposing mutual nuclear disarmament
choices.outcome: From the edge of war you reach instead for the unthinkable: peace. The hawks call it weakness; history may call it sanity.

events.title: Every Window a Mirror
events.scene: The cameras are everywhere now, and they recognize faces, gaits, even moods. A unified national system can find anyone in the country in under a minute, your security chief boasts, and predict where they'll be tomorrow. He calls it safety. The dictionary calls it something older.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real expansions of facial recognition, biometric tracking, and predictive policing technologies. The fully integrated national surveillance grid depicted exceeds current deployed systems.

choices.text: Build the total surveillance grid and watch everyone
choices.outcome: Privacy becomes a quaint memory and you become the eye that never closes. Crime falls; so does anything resembling freedom.

choices.text: Ban mass biometric surveillance and enshrine a right to privacy
choices.outcome: You smash the cameras before they smash the country. The security chief sulks; the citizens breathe in the dark, unwatched.

choices.text: Allow it only with warrants and strict oversight
choices.outcome: You keep the tool and chain it to a judge. A compromise that satisfies no one and protects almost everyone.

events.title: The Last Hard Question
events.scene: A lone outlet still runs investigations you don't like, the last newsroom that hasn't either folded or fallen in line. Its latest story is unflattering and, infuriatingly, accurate. Your lawyers have three ways to make it disappear, and your better angels have one reason not to.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real global trends of press consolidation, libel-law pressure, and harassment of independent journalists. The depicted near-total capture of the press is dramatized.

choices.text: Sue it into oblivion and seize its servers
choices.outcome: The last hard question goes quiet. The silence is total, and somehow louder than any headline ever was.

choices.text: Let them publish and answer the story on the merits
choices.outcome: You take the hit and leave the press standing. A leader who can be questioned, it turns out, is a rarer thing than a crown.

choices.text: Quietly buy it and bend it from the inside
choices.outcome: You don't kill the watchdog; you adopt it. Within a month it is wagging its tail at every press conference.

events.title: A New Founding
events.scene: A loyal supermajority offers to rewrite the founding document itself. They've drafted articles that would bend the whole apparatus toward a single will, or, in a stranger draft on the same desk, toward the broadest sharing of power the nation has ever tried. The pen is in your hand. The ink is permanent.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real historical cases of constitutional replacement used both to entrench autocrats and, more rarely, to expand democratic participation. The specific U.S. rewrite is imagined.

choices.text: Rewrite it to enshrine permanent personal rule
choices.outcome: The republic's bones are rebuilt around your spine. Every clause now points, sooner or later, at you.

choices.text: Rewrite it to radically expand democracy and rights
choices.outcome: You spend your power buying everyone else more of it. The most absolute act of your life is giving the absolutism away.

choices.text: Refuse to touch it and let the old document stand
choices.outcome: You leave the founding alone, flaws and all. Sometimes the most radical move is to change nothing.

events.title: Crowned in All But Name
events.scene: The portrait painters have started adding laurel. A constitutional 'reform' redefines the presidency as a lifetime stewardship 'in times of permanent emergency.' You stop correcting aides who slip and call you something grander than Mister President.
events.researchNote: Speculative extrapolation from real historical patterns where leaders used emergency powers and constitutional rewrites to entrench themselves indefinitely (a recurring pattern in democratic backsliding). The U.S. application here is imagined satire.

choices.text: Accept the 'permanent stewardship' and drop the pretense
choices.outcome: The republic exhales its last constitutional breath. You are emperor in everything but the spelling, and the spelling can wait.

choices.text: Keep the republican costume while wielding total power
choices.outcome: You keep the elections, the flags, the speeches, and none of the limits. The form survives; the substance is yours alone.

choices.text: Refuse the crown and restore the term limit you erased
choices.outcome: At the very door of empire, you turn around. The laurel goes back in its box; the republic, gasping, lives.

events.title: Elon Takes the Oath; Donald Watches from a Sky-Box
events.scene: On inauguration day Musk stands at the Capitol, one hand on a copy of the Constitution and the other on the memory of six billion dollars of his own money that put him here. You watch from a glass-paneled suite above the crowd. The crowd below belongs to him now. You tell yourself a tycoon without a throne can still own the century. You are not certain you believe it yet.
events.researchNote: Extrapolated. Directly dramatizes the musk.json timeline's wk_musk_takes_power event (2037) from Donald's point of view, imagining the moment the role flip becomes concrete and irreversible.

choices.text: Toast Musk publicly. "He learned everything from me."
choices.outcome: You claim credit for the man who replaced you, and the market believes it. The brand appreciates even as the power drains south.

choices.text: Say nothing. Let the cameras find your face and read what they want.
choices.outcome: The silence speaks volumes. Your face, seen in three thousand pixels on every screen, launches a thousand think-pieces about what you are planning next.

choices.text: Leave the ceremony early and announce a trillion-dollar commercial venture from the lobby.
choices.outcome: You steal the news cycle from Musk's own inauguration. He notices. This is how you operate now — not from the Oval but from whatever room has the best camera angle.

events.title: The Empire vs. the Presidency
events.scene: A year into Musk's term and the score is visible: he commands the nuclear codes; you command a commercial empire that touches more daily American lives than any agency he runs. Your real-estate, media, and licensing machine generates more federal tax than half the departments Musk is dismantling. The {head_of_state} needs the economy you represent. You have found a leverage point that doesn't require the Oval.
events.researchNote: Extrapolated. Imagines the structural dynamic of the role-flip — a commercially dominant Trump coexisting with a politically dominant Musk — and the institutional leverage each camp holds over the other.

choices.text: Threaten to move capital offshore unless Musk's policies serve your interests.
choices.outcome: You weaponize your balance sheet against the Oval Office. Musk flinches on one regulation and the financial press writes about 'Trump's shadow presidency.' Not bad for a man watching from the sky-box.

choices.text: Back Musk's Mars and AI programs. Let the government make you rich; the brand goes interplanetary.
choices.outcome: Your commercial empire plugs into the fastest-growing market in human history: the interplanetary economy. You do not need to be president when you own the import-export license for Mars.

choices.text: Announce a 2040 primary challenge. The base never stopped belonging to you.
choices.outcome: The base ignites. You are eighty-something and still the most dangerous man in every room you enter. Musk's internal polling immediately turns ugly. The rematch is on.

events.title: The Red Planet Needs a Bank
events.scene: Musk's Mars colony is growing — one hundred and twelve colonists, a pressurized hab, and an economy with no currency yet. SpaceX has the rockets; it does not have the financial infrastructure. Musk's chief of staff calls Mar-a-Lago at two in the morning. They need someone who knows how to build a branded commercial ecosystem from nothing. They need you.
events.researchNote: Extrapolated. Imagines an interplanetary commercial opportunity emerging from Musk's Mars program that requires Trump's brand-building and transactional expertise, dramatizing the tycoon-patron relationship between the two in the flip scenario.

choices.text: Demand naming rights, equity, and a full licensing deal before you send a single architect.
choices.outcome: The first Martian city has gold lettering above the airlock. The astronauts joke about it; the balance sheet does not.

choices.text: Build the financial infrastructure as an equal partner. Musk gets the flag; you get the economy.
choices.outcome: You build the Martian dollar and the Martian trade routes and the Martian hotels. He gets a planet named in his honor; you get a monopoly on everything that lives on it.

choices.text: Hang up. You don't work for the man who took your throne.
choices.outcome: You hang up and the satisfaction is immediate. The resentment is also immediate. Mars will have a financial system built by someone else, and you will have the small pleasure of having said no.

events.title: The Base Remembers Who It Loves
events.scene: Two years into Musk's presidency and his approval numbers with your voters are structurally soft. They follow his rockets but not his cadence. At every rally you hold for your commercial ventures, thousands arrive with red hats — old ones, the originals. Musk's pollsters are measuring the gap. The question of who actually owns this movement is unresolved, and you intend to keep it that way.
events.researchNote: Extrapolated. Imagines the continuing political asymmetry between Musk as president and Trump as the loyalty-anchor of the movement that put both of them in their respective positions.

choices.text: Hold the rallies, keep the base warm, and let Musk wonder what you are building.
choices.outcome: The rallies become a shadow government of mood. Musk reads the crowd sizes and adjusts his policy accordingly. You have not returned to politics; you have become the weather.

choices.text: Endorse Musk for a second term. Lock in your patron status in exchange for the commercial concessions.
choices.outcome: You deliver the base like a package and extract the invoice. Musk wins re-election by the margin your endorsement provided, and you collect accordingly. The arrangement is transactional and stable, which is the closest thing to comfortable you have felt since leaving office.

choices.text: Quietly position a loyalist candidate against Musk in the primary. Let the base choose.
choices.outcome: You install a proxy and sit back. Musk understands what it means and the war begins in earnest, fought in polling booths and rally attendances and nightly news segments about who the movement really belongs to.
